title: Writing hypotheses to test the difference of means
description: Practice writing the null and alternative hypothesis in a two-sample t test for the difference of means.
topic_path: Testing for the difference of two population means
Writing hypotheses to test the difference of means

assessment_items.question_content:
Market researchers conducted a study comparing the salaries of managers at a large nationwide retail store. The researchers obtained salary and demographic data for a random sample of managers.

The researchers calculated the average salary of the men in the sample and the average salary of the women in the sample. They want to test if managers who are men have a higher average salary than managers who are women. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of these is the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Paired test or two-sample test?

Consider the design of the study.

Use a **paired test** if the study paired subjects in some way, or if the study compares two measurements on each subject.

Use a **two-sample test** if the data were produced from two independent groups.

In this study, the researchers aren't pairing the men in any way with the women.

Since the salaries came from independent groups in this study, we should use a **two-sample $t$ test** to compare the average salary in each group.

###Alternative hypothesis $(\text{H}_\text{a})$ for a two-sample test

Are they testing if the average from one group is higher than the other? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 > \mu_2$ or $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 - \mu_2 > 0$.

Are they testing for a difference between the groups? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$ or $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 - \mu_2 \neq 0$.

They want to test if managers who are **men** have a **higher** average salary than managers who are **women**.

So $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{men} >\mu_\text{women}$ would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis.

###Answer

Of the choices, the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis is a **two-sample $t$ test with $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{men}>\mu_\text{women}$**.

assessment_items.question_content:
The managers of a fast food chain want their products to be as similar as possible across locations. They suspect that the burgers at their Albuquerque branch have bigger patties than the burgers at the Santa Fe branch, so they take a sample of $7$ patties from each restaurant and measure their weights (in grams):

||||||||
- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -:
Albuquerque | $110$ | $111$ | $110$ | $113$ | $111$ | $112$ | $106$
Santa Fe | $107$ | $111$ | $110$ | $108$ | $109$ | $110$ | $109$

The managers want to test if the patties in the Albuquerque branch have a higher average weight than the patties in the Santa Fe branch. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of these is the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Paired test or two-sample test?

Consider the design of the study.

Use a **paired test** if the study paired subjects in some way, or if the study compares two measurements on each subject.

Use a **two-sample test** if the data were produced from two independent groups.

In this study, the managers aren't pairing the Albuquerque patties in any way with the Santa Fe patties.

Since the weights came from independent groups in this study, we should use a **two-sample $t$ test** to compare the average weight in each group.

###Alternative hypothesis $(\text{H}_\text{a})$ for a two-sample test

Are they testing if the average from one group is higher than the other? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 > \mu_2$ or $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 - \mu_2 > 0$.

Are they testing for a difference between the groups? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$ or $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 - \mu_2 \neq 0$.

They want to test if patties in **Albuquerque** have a **higher** average weight than patties in **Santa Fe**.

So $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{Albuquerque} >\mu_\text{Santa Fe}$ would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis.

###Answer

Of the choices, the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis is a **two-sample $t$ test with $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{Albuquerque}>\mu_\text{Santa Fe}$**.

assessment_items.question_content:
A teacher wants to test whether his students will perform better on their exam if the room where they take the exam is cooled to a freezing temperature.

On the day of an exam, he randomly assigned his class into two groups. One group took the exam under regular conditions and one group took the exam in a freezing cold room. After the exam, the teacher calculated the average score in each group. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of these is the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Paired test or two-sample test?

Consider the design of the study.

Use a **paired test** if the study paired subjects in some way, or if the study compares two measurements on each subject.

Use a **two-sample test** if the data were produced from two independent groups.

In this study, the teacher isn't pairing the students who took the exam in the freezing cold room in any way with the students who took the exam under regular conditions.

Since the scores came from independent groups in this study, we should use a **two-sample $t$ test** to compare the average score in each group.

###Alternative hypothesis $(\text{H}_\text{a})$ for a two-sample test

Are they testing if the average from one group is higher than the other? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 > \mu_2$ or $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 - \mu_2 > 0$.

Are they testing for a difference between the groups? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$ or $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 - \mu_2 \neq 0$.

They want to test if students who took the exam in a **freezing** room have a **higher** average score than students who took the exam in **regular** conditions.

So $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{freeze} >\mu_\text{regular}$ would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis.

###Answer

Of the choices, the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis is a **two-sample $t$ test with $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{freeze}>\mu_\text{regular}$**.

assessment_items.question_content:
A study had subjects read a passage and take a comprehension test on what they read. The researchers randomly assigned subjects so that half of them listened to music with lyrics while reading, while the other half listened to music without lyrics while reading. Here are their scores on the comprehension test:

||||||
- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -:
Music with lyrics | $75$ | $62$ | $65$ | $41$ | $54$
Music without lyrics | $64$ | $71$ | $88$ | $77$ | $62$

The researchers want to test if these results represent a significant difference in the average score between the groups. Assume that the necessary conditions for inference were met.

**Which of these is the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Paired test or two-sample test?

Consider the design of the study.

Use a **paired test** if the study paired subjects in some way, or if the study compares two measurements on each subject.

Use a **two-sample test** if the data were produced from two independent groups.

In this study, the researchers aren't pairing subjects who listened to music with lyrics in any way with the subjects who listened to music without lyrics.

Since the scores came from independent groups in this study, we should use a **two-sample $t$ test** to compare the average score in each group.

###Alternative hypothesis $(\text{H}_\text{a})$ for a two-sample test

Are they testing if the average from one group is higher than the other? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 > \mu_2$ or $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 - \mu_2 > 0$.

Are they testing for a difference between the groups? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$ or $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 - \mu_2 \neq 0$.

The researchers want to test if these results represent a significant **difference** in the average score between the groups.

So $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{lyrics} \neq \mu_\text{without}$ would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis.

###Answer

Of the choices, the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis is a **two-sample $t$ test with $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{lyrics} \neq \mu_\text{without}$**.

assessment_items.question_content:
Owen lives in New York City and his brother Karson lives in Boston. They both complained about the high prices of avocado in their neighborhoods, so they decided to test whether there's a difference in the average price of avocado.

Each of them took a random sample of shops in their neighborhoods and got the price of avocado from each shop. Then, they calculated the average price in each group. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of these is the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Paired test or two-sample test?

Consider the design of the study.

Use a **paired test** if the study paired subjects in some way, or if the study compares two measurements on each subject.

Use a **two-sample test** if the data were produced from two independent groups.

In this study, the brothers aren't pairing prices of avocado in shops in New York City with prices of avocado in shops in Boston.

Since the prices came from independent groups in this study, we should use a **two-sample $t$ test** to compare the average price in each group.

###Alternative hypothesis $(\text{H}_\text{a})$ for a two-sample test

Are they testing if the average from one group is higher than the other? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 > \mu_2$ or $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 - \mu_2 > 0$.

Are they testing for a difference between the groups? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$ or $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 - \mu_2 \neq 0$.

The brothers want to test if these results represent a significant **difference** in the average price between the groups.

So $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{New York City} \neq \mu_\text{Boston}$ would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis.

###Answer

Of the choices, the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis is a **two-sample $t$ test with $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{New York City} \neq \mu_\text{Boston}$**.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lea is wondering whether there is a height difference between right-handed and left-handed students at her school. She takes a random sample of students and measures their heights. Then she calculates the average height of the right-handed people in the sample and the average height of the left-handed people in the sample.

Lea wants to test if her results represent a significant difference in the average height between the groups. Assume that the necessary conditions for inference were met.

**Which of these is the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Paired test or two-sample test?

Consider the design of the study.

Use a **paired test** if the study paired subjects in some way, or if the study compares two measurements on each subject.

Use a **two-sample test** if the data were produced from two independent groups.

In this study, Lea isn't pairing right-handed students in any way with left-handed students.

Since the heights came from independent groups in this study, we should use a **two-sample $t$ test** to compare the average height in each group.

###Alternative hypothesis $(\text{H}_\text{a})$ for a two-sample test

Are they testing if the average from one group is higher than the other? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 > \mu_2$ or $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 - \mu_2 > 0$.

Are they testing for a difference between the groups? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$ or $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 - \mu_2 \neq 0$.

Lea wants to test if these results represent a significant **difference** in the average height between the groups.

So $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{right-handed} \neq \mu_\text{left-handed}$ would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis.

###Answer

Of the choices, the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis is a **two-sample $t$ test with $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{right-handed} \neq \mu_\text{left-handed}$**.

assessment_items.question_content:
An exercise scientist wanted to test the effectiveness of a new program designed to increase the flexibility of senior citizens. They recruited participants and rated their flexibility according to a standard scale before starting the program. The participants all went through the program and had their flexibility rated again after a month.

The scientist wants to test if the flexibility ratings are significantly higher after a month of the program. Assume that these participants can be considered a representative sample and that all other necessary conditions for inference were met.

**Which of these is the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Paired test or two-sample test?

Consider the design of the study.

Use a **paired test** if the study paired subjects in some way, or if the study compares two measurements on each subject.

Use a **two-sample test** if the data were produced from two independent groups.

In this study, each participant produced two measurements (their before and after ratings). Those measurements are almost certainly dependent in some way, so it wouldn't be appropriate to treat the before ratings as being independent from the after ratings.

Since the ratings were paired for each participant, we should use a **paired $t$ test** on the average difference between the before and after ratings.

###Alternative hypothesis $(\text{H}_\text{a})$ for a paired test

Are they testing if one set of measurements is, on average, higher than the other? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_\text{difference} > 0$.

Are they testing for a difference between the sets? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_\text{difference} \neq 0$.

The scientist wants to test if the flexibility ratings are significantly **higher** after a month of the program.

So $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_{\text{after}-\text{before}}>0$ would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis. If the after scores are typically higher, then the average difference $(\text{after}-\text{before})$ will be positive.

###Answer

Of the choices, the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis is a **paired $t$ test with $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_{\text{after}-\text{before}}>0$**.

assessment_items.question_content:
The town where Lily lives has two main road intersections. Lily, a transportation engineer, suspects that one intersection is busier than the other. She sent two of her employees to track the number of cars that pass through each intersection at $5$ different times of the day. Here are the number of cars passing through each intersection at each point in time:
 
Time | $6\text{ am}$ | $9\text{ am}$ | $12\text{ pm}$ | $4\text{ pm}$ | $7\text{ pm}$
- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Intersection A** | $28$ | $34$ | $24$ | $33$ | $27$
**Intersection B** | $26$ | $32$ | $21$ | $34$ | $26$

Lily wants to test if these results suggest intersection **A** is busier than intersection **B**. Assume that the necessary conditions for inference were met.

**Which of these is the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Paired test or two-sample test?

Consider the design of the study.

Use a **paired test** if the study paired subjects in some way, or if the study compares two measurements on each subject.

Use a **two-sample test** if the data were produced from two independent groups.

In this study, each time produced two measurements (number of cars in intersection **A** and number of cars in intersection **B**). Those measurements are almost certainly dependent in some way, so it wouldn't be appropriate to treat the intersection **A** measurements as being independent from the intersection **B** measurements.

Since the measurements were paired for time, we should use a **paired $t$ test** on the average difference between the measurements.

###Alternative hypothesis $(\text{H}_\text{a})$ for a paired test

Are they testing if one set of measurements is, on average, higher than the other? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_\text{difference} > 0$.

Are they testing for a difference between the sets? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_\text{difference} \neq 0$.

Lily wants to test if intersection **A** is significantly **busier** than intersection **B**.

So $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_{\text{A}-\text{B}}>0$ would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis. If the intersection **A** measurements are typically higher, then the average difference $(\text{A}-\text{B})$ will be positive.

###Answer

Of the choices, the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis is a **paired $t$ test with $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_{\text{A}-\text{B}}>0$**.

assessment_items.question_content:
The Olympic running team of Freedonia has always used "Zeppo's" running shoes, but their manager suspects "Harpo's" shoes can produce better results (lower times). The manager has $6$ runners each run two laps: one lap wearing "Zeppo's" and another lap wearing "Harpo's." Each runner flips a coin to determine which shoes they wear first.

The manager wants to test if their times when wearing  "Harpo's" are significantly lower than their times when they wear "Zeppo's." Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**Which of these is the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Paired test or two-sample test?

Consider the design of the study.

Use a **paired test** if the study paired subjects in some way, or if the study compares two measurements on each subject.

Use a **two-sample test** if the data were produced from two independent groups.

In this study, each runner produced two measurements. Those measurements are almost certainly dependent in some way, so it wouldn't be appropriate to treat the "Harpo's" shoes measurements as being independent from the "Zeppo's" shoes measurements.

Since the times are paired for each runner, we should use a **paired $t$ test** on the average difference between the times.

###Alternative hypothesis $(\text{H}_\text{a})$ for a paired test

Are they testing if one set of measurements is, on average, higher than the other? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_\text{difference} > 0$.

Are they testing for a difference between the sets? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_\text{difference} \neq 0$.

The manager wants to test if the times are significantly **lower** when wearing "Harpo's" shoes.

So $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_{\text{Zeppo's}-\text{Harpo's}}>0$ would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis. If the times are significantly **lower** when wearing "Harpo's" shoes, then the average difference $(\text{Zeppo's}-\text{Harpo's})$ will be positive.

###Answer

Of the choices, the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis is a **paired $t$ test with $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_{\text{Zeppo's}-\text{Harpo's}}>0$**.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two friends were curious if it was faster to use the drive-thru or order at the counter at their favorite fast food restaurant. For $5$ different visits, one of them ordered at the counter while the other used the drive-thru (determined by a coin toss). Each person ordered the same meal at every visit. Here are the times (in minutes) it took them to get their orders:
 
|||||||
- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -: | -:
Counter | $5$ | $8$ | $10$ | $7$ | $7$ |
Drive-thru | $3$ | $10$ | $7$ | $7$ | $8$ |

They want to test if these results suggest a significant difference in the average time between ordering at the counter and ordering in the drive-thru. Assume that the necessary conditions for inference were met.

**Which of these is the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Paired test or two-sample test?

Consider the design of the study.

Use a **paired test** if the study involved paired subjects/units in some way, or if the study compares two measurements taken on each subject/unit.

Use a **two-sample test** if the data were produced from two independent groups.

In this study, each visit produced two measurements. Those measurements are almost certainly dependent in some way, so it wouldn't be appropriate to treat the counter times as being independent from the drive-thru times.

Since the times were paired in each visit, we should use a **paired $t$ test** on the average difference between the times.

###Alternative hypothesis $(\text{H}_\text{a})$ for a paired test

Are they testing if measurements from one group are, on average, higher than the other? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_\text{difference} > 0$.

Are they testing for a difference between the groups? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_\text{difference} \neq 0$.

They want to test if these results suggest a significant **difference** in the average time between ordering at the counter and ordering in the drive-thru.

So $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{difference}\neq0$ would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis.

###Answer

Of the choices, the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis is a **paired $t$ test with $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{difference}\neq0$**.

assessment_items.question_content:
Dan, an office manager, wants to replace the catering company in charge of the office lunch. The new company promises the same quality of food at a smaller cost.

Before he makes the switch between companies, Dan sends out a satisfaction survey of the old company to a sample of $30$ employees. After a month with the new company, he sends out the same satisfaction survey to the same group of employees. Each survey produces a satisfaction rating.

Dan wants to test if the results suggest a significant difference in the average satisfaction rating between the old and the new catering companies. Assume that the necessary conditions for inference were met.

**Which of these is the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Paired test or two-sample test?

Consider the design of the study.

Use a **paired test** if the study involved paired subjects/units in some way, or if the study compares two measurements taken on each subject/unit.

Use a **two-sample test** if the data were produced from two independent groups.

In this study, each employee produces two ratings. Those ratings are almost certainly dependent in some way, so it wouldn't be appropriate to treat the satisfactions ratings of company **A** as being independent from the satisfactions ratings of company **B**.

Since each employee produces a pair of ratings, we should use a **paired $t$ test** on the average difference between the ratings.

###Alternative hypothesis $(\text{H}_\text{a})$ for a paired test

Are they testing if measurements from one group are, on average, higher than the other? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_\text{difference} > 0$.

Are they testing for a difference between the groups? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_\text{difference} \neq 0$.

Dan wants to test if these results suggest a significant **difference** in the average satisfaction rating between the two companies.

So $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{difference}\neq0$ would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis.

###Answer

Of the choices, the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis is a **paired $t$ test with $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{difference}\neq0$**.

assessment_items.question_content:
Apgar score is a score between $0$ and $10$ that gives a measure of the physical condition of a newborn infant. Researchers collected the Apgar scores of $20$ pairs of identical twins.

The researchers wanted to test if their results suggest a significant difference in the Apgar score between the first-born twin and the second-born twin. Assume that the necessary conditions for inference were met.

**Which of these is the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Paired test or two-sample test?

Consider the design of the study.

Use a **paired test** if the study involved paired subjects/units in some way, or if the study compares two measurements taken on each subject/unit.

Use a **two-sample test** if the data were produced from two independent groups.

In this study, each pair of twins produced two scores. Those scores are almost certainly dependent in some way, so it wouldn't be appropriate to treat the first-born scores as being independent from the second-born scores.

We should use a **paired $t$ test** on the average difference between the first-borns and the second-borns.

###Alternative hypothesis $(\text{H}_\text{a})$ for a paired test

Are they testing if measurements from one group are, on average, higher than the other? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_\text{difference} > 0$.

Are they testing for a difference between the groups? If so, their alternative hypothesis should look something like $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_\text{difference} \neq 0$.

They want to test if these results suggest a significant **difference** in the average score between first-borns and second-borns.

So $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{difference}\neq0$ would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis.

###Answer

Of the choices, the most appropriate test and alternative hypothesis is a **paired $t$ test with $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_\text{difference}\neq0$**.
